Variation over 5-year intervals in built-up properties between 1820–2000* [^fig-2] ![](figures/global_building_atlas_heigth_maps.jpg){#fig-gba-overview fig-cap="Overview of the resulting GlobalBuildingAtlas dataset, consisting of global building polygons, building height map, and LoD1 models. Base images © Google Maps. [^fig-3]"} *figure 3. [^fig-3] Global and complete dataset of building polygons, heights and LoD1 :::: :::: {.right-column} # The modern inmensity of the urban In pre-modern times, our relationship with the urban environment could be engaged—if not directly manipulated—through simple locomotion. Wandering served as a sufficient means to experience and intuitively assimilate the urban tissue. Utilizing *Freundschuh and Egenhofer’s spatial taxonomy*[^text-1], the historical city can be classified at the _environmental scale_: a dimension where the designer was physically subjected to the city's diverse perceptual cues over time. As cities expanded, their immense scale began to represent more than just a practical complication of transport; it signaled an ontological withdrawal of the urban phenomenon and an epistemological shift toward highly abstract ways of conceiving the city. Although the technological evolution of transport (cars, trains, and planes) alongside remote sensing (satellites and surveying equipment) allowed citizens to maintain a relationship with the urban environment by drastically reducing the time required to traverse it, this very mediation fractured our perception. *Trapped in this discontinuous transit and reliant upon abstract representations, we ultimately lost the capacity to relate to the city's concrete, physical aspect.*[^text-2] Across the spam of two centuries this change in magnitude widrew the experience of the city from our sensibilities as we knew it. The proportions of urban growth are overwhelming, both demographically and spatially. *After reaching a population of 500 million in 1543, it took nearly seven centuries (260 years) to reach one billion in 1803. From point forward, the pace accelerated: 125 years later, in 1928, the population doubled to two billion; and merely 47 years after that, in 1975, it surpassed four billion. In less than half a century, the figure quadrupled. By 2024, we have already surpassed 8 billion, and projections indicate that before 2100, we will reach between 10 and 11 billion.*[^text-3] This growth had transformed the city of past century into a metropolis, and it has assembled them in a megalopolis and nested finaly in a *ecumenopolis*[^text-4]. The urban enviromented enters now partialy in our lifes, closer to climate and other worldly events, it has become psicolgicably increasingly inaccesible in its totality by pure sensible intuition, and the design medium trougth wich we concive them have turn to virtual domains. What exactly was lost during this transformation? How did we build the modern city if not through physical intuition? Which design methodologies triumphed in this technological revolution, and which were left behind? Why can intuition not be conveyed through modern design mediums? How might we trace a genealogy of intuitive design mediums to reclaim this lost connection? :::: ::::: ::: {.cell execution_count=1} ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-display} ``` Unable to display output for mime type(s): text/html ``` Evolución de la población por ciudad (Top 5 destacadas) ::: ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-display} ``` Unable to display output for mime type(s): text/html ``` ::: ::: *figure 4.
